speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 4. Today's date is November 26, 1995. This is an interview with Clara Posner. You were telling me the story with the Russian who only gave you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, what shall I do with 180 ruble? I have no bread, nothing. And there was a Russian lady. With the Russian soldiers she came, a lady. She said to me, once, you know, that the Russian-- what you don't steal, you haven't got. You know what it-- [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. She told me. I said, OK, now I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then after he gave me the 180 ruble, I said, I'm going to do something, you know? He brought me material every day. And I start cutting. I said, there is all kind of soldiers, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. They don't care if the sleeve is going to be an inch shorter. Who cares? The soldiers-- or the pants a little shorter. They don't care. They don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I cut it everything an inch shorter, the sleeves shorter, the shirt shorter a little bit, the pants a little bit shorter. And through the day, I got about a yard, maybe two yard material. I went to the ladies' room. [POINTING] When I came home, I went to the lady-- the lady what gave my mother the leaves from the beets. And I brought her this. She gave me bread. She gave me butter. She gave me milk. She even gave me a chicken. She made the debt. I didn't. She gave me everything. I said, nah, I don't gonna-- you got 180 rubles. Now I got everything. Now you know what I did. I did-- I steal. [LAUGHS] You steal. If you're hungry, you steal. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were doing this work, you were living in your grandfather's house?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. What was-- what was life like for you? Was it-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was it dangerous at all during this period?

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were living there, was it still dangerous at all for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: --the Russians didn't-- no, it wasn't dangerous.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was difficult to find food.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I told you, when the war was finished, in-- and they, they went [INAUDIBLE]. We can go over there. But I said, I don't want to be here. What is there about Russia? Nothing is here. I, I know I got relatives in America, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: In days-- for Russland, for Russia, I know I, I wouldn't go no place. From Poland, maybe, you know. And I came. I registered. I want to go to Poland. And I took the little girl with me, yeah. And then one day, I-- in Poland-- Chelm, that was in Chelm. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Polish soldiers came home from the war and-- without legs, without arms, you know, and blaming them and chasing the Jewish people. And they went in one-- in one-- we're here. And there was a, a family-- a doctor and a wife and a little girl. The little girl wasn't that time. And I was-- and they shot the wife and the doctor with the wife. And I said that time to my husband, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. That's not the place for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Who-- I'm sorry, who shot them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we escaped. We went that time to Lodz. You know Lodz? We went to Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from Lodz, I don't know if we met Jewish people. And they told us from Germany, there is a place. From there, you can-- you can-- you can make it to America maybe, he said. And we went from Lodz to Germany to Waldenburg. You hear this? Waldenburg. Yeah. How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. By train-- we went. I don't know. By train, by that-- we went. It took us a-- weeks, a few weeks, but we came there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we've been there in Waldenburg. And there, I met a man. He told me, you know, something-- somebody told him that it wasn't far from Czechoslovakia, there where we've been. He said, in Czechoslovakia, somebody told me that there is a place they come from Israel and they-- to get, I don't know, Jewish people. And they're going to take them away someplace. We hear this. And we, we went there. I don't know, by train, something, we went to Czechoslovakia. And we-- the other man, too. And we-- they-- somebody told us, there is a house. We came in this house. And there was a lot of Jewish people. And they told us they're waiting. Somebody will come, and they'll take us. And this was, I don't know. Two men came. Yeah, and they took us. We didn't know. But they told us in the train-- they took us with open trains, you know, open trains. And they told not to speak nothing, not Jewish, not Polish, nothing. Because, he said, they're going to come, some people, to see. Because they told us is-- we is not Jewish. It's-- they told us that we are Greek people, Greek. We're Greek. And we didn't spoke nothing. And they came. We saw them. And they talked to us. And we didn't answer. We didn't know nothing. And they took us to Vienna. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in-- I think it should-- about the end of 1945, I think--

speaker: interviewee
text: --to Vienna. We slept over in Vienna there in a hospital. In a hospital, I remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they said it's a hospital. And the next day they took us away in a different place. And believe me, I don't know the names. And we've been there a few weeks in a place. They gave us-- they gave us food. Not much, but we couldn't die. We got food. Then, from there, they took us in-- in DP camp in Germany, in Wetzlar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- my son was born there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I named him after my oldest brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: In DP camp, Displaced Persons camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And from there, I don't know-- I find out I-- my father got two sisters in Des Moines, Iowa, in America. I remember something because my mother used to send paper, you know, to there. And I used to write the addresses. And I remember. I remember the name of my uncle, the first and the second name. And I remember he got a store or a shop for iron. That's all I remember. I [LAUGHS] wrote a letter. I said I was alive with my husband, and I have a child. I would like to come to you. Take me there. I am in the Displaced Persons camp. And I had the address. Nathan, I was-- the name was Nathan. The second name was [? Fingeret, ?] Company [INAUDIBLE]. That's all what I remember. [LAUGHS] The letter was came. And he answered me. And they sent me a certificate. And they took me to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: When, when did you arrive in America?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. You arrived in New Orleans?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived in New Orleans. That's where they took the picture for my little boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, he was a year and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: How, how did you get here? On a--

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was with you? Your husband?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What, what, what had happened to your-- to other family members?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know I have nobody. I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. OK, what did you do when you arrived in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: To America? I've been in New Orleans, no, Des Moines, Iowa. From New Orleans, there is-- there was a Joint or something, HIAS, they took us by train. Took me a day and a half to come from New Orleans to Des Moines, Iowa. I went from New Orleans to Chicago, and from Chicago to Des Moines, Iowa. But I was by my uncle there only two weeks. I didn't want to be there. I didn't like it there. It was so cold there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I came exactly the night for Purim. You know Purim? I came exactly the night for Purim. I came there. And I didn't like it. First of all, I didn't got that job. And I didn't like it. That is just a place-- you know, it's a funny place there. It's-- my uncle told me, it's only the cowboys here, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you want to have a chicken for Shabbos, they got-- we had to go to the village and buy a chicken, and go to the-- to the [NON-ENGLISH] to [NON-ENGLISH]. There was nothing. I said, I wouldn't be here. But I remember my mother got two brothers in Brooklyn. And I remember. And I send a letter. And they sent me right away two tickets. And I came to Brooklyn. It was exactly a week before Passover.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. And what did you do? What did you do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. I couldn't find-- my husband couldn't find a job-- nothing. I went some-- and my husband went Shabbos to the synagogue. And somebody told him that here is a Joint, HIAS. You know what it is? People, what-- they took care of the refugees, something like this-- was in Brooklyn. And he gave me the address. And I went there. I went there with my husband, with the baby. I went there. And I said, OK. He said, we are not responsible. Because they took old people to America, the HIAS, the Joint. But we didn't take you. Your, your uncle took you. He's responsible for you. I said, yeah, it's true. He's responsible. But they are old people [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't be there. They have nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just told him, they're old people. They have nothing. I have no job there. What shall I do there? Go begging, something? He said, no, all right. And they ask us-- we came there in the HIAS. You don't know what it means HIAS? Joint? They took care of the refugees. Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came there. And I've been there for a few weeks. And then my husband got a Tante in Boston. And I gave him the-- he remember. He gave her to the paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she came. She came to us. And she took us to Providence, Rhode Island. And she find a job for my husband. And we came there right away.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came-- I remember it was Thursday night. Friday morning, my husband was-- right away went to the-- to work. He worked in a kosher butcher, big butcher shop. He worked there--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Providence, Rhode Island?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, I've been there-- my husband, I was 40 years. And my husband was sick, and he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. When, when did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was left with my boy, who was that time, seven year old. That's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know what to do. I didn't-- I didn't know if I can collect Social Security. I didn't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was so green-- four years. Then the boss, the butcher, came to my house. And he asked me, how old my husband, this and that. I said, what for you need it? He's dead. He said, he worked for me four years. I paid for him. You'll collect Social Security. Otherwise, I didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's was-- and then I started collecting Social Security. And I was in Providence-- when I came four years with my husband. I was, after my husband died, I was seven or eight years, about seven years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my second husband, he was my cousin. He-- somebody told him that I lived there. And he came to me. He was alone. And he took me here, and we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got married again when?

speaker: interviewee
text: After seven years when my husband died. It was, I think, you know, I remember it was this year when they shot President Kennedy.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think '63, yeah. I was with him 26 years, with my second husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was your-- he was your cousin?

speaker: interviewee
text: His grandfather, my grandfather were brothers. He came. And he told me to come to him. He's-- he took me here to New York. And I have a few cousins here in New York, too. And we got married. And we was-- we were living. I was with him 26 years. And then he got a stroke, and he died. He's 80 year when he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what have you done while you've been here? Have you worked?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah? What, what have you done?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked in a shop. I worked in a shop.

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned to make pockets to boys' pants, a pocket maker.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I worked with that man 18 years. And all of a sudden, I don't know how he died. He was operated for the kidneys or something. And he died. And the wife sold the shop. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. What was the name of the shop? The name of the shop.

speaker: interviewee
text: His name-- he was-- he-- the-- he, he was [? Meyer, ?] [? Meyer. ?] The name was [? Meyer. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been involved with Jewish organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I belong to the Hadassah. I belong to the sisterhood. We have a sisterhood for another synagogue.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have a Sisterhood to the Hadassah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I go there-- every Shabbos, I go down in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. How do you think your experiences in the war have influenced your identity as, as a Jewish person? How do you think it's influenced your identity?

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you feel about being Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] How do I feel about being Jewish? Do you know what did-- they did to Jewish people? That's all what they did. I fear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, here, is plenty of anti-Semitism here, too. You know something? I'm not ashamed to say in America, it's plenty. Plenty, I know. Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: They don't like Jews here in America. Don't you know? You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why I survived? God, only God-- I believe in this. It wasn't my time to die, I think so. That's what I believe. I used to say but-- to my husband sometime. You know, sometime, I said, I don't believe I survived. I don't know how. It wasn't my time. I believe in God. That's it. It wasn't my time.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I have survived. I have a son, with beautiful grandsons.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sons? My son's name is Suchar. I named him after my older brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandson's name is Lee. He is named after his grandfather, my first husband, Lebel.

speaker: interviewee
text: The other is named after my daughter-in-law's mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. And what's your daughter-in-law's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pauline. She, she-- her parents is the same thing, survived like I. But she, she-- born in America. She-- born here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Is there anything else that I haven't asked you that you'd like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. [LAUGHS] I think it's plenty.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know, you know? I don't know how I survived. Believe me. It's [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. They tried this. I was lying in the [NON-ENGLISH] in the rain. It, it comes out, the doctor told me-- it comes after the 20 years, you know, I've been--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I'm not so healthy. Maybe I look like. But I am not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any message or statement that you have for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: What should I say, don't be Jewish? What should I say to them? They are Jews. [LAUGHS] What can I say to them?

speaker: interviewer
text: God bless you. That's all. Be alive until your time comes to end it here. What can I say to them? What would you say to the Jewish people? What can you say? Nothing? You didn't got the same as I. I know what I've got. Nobody likes the Jewish people. You want to know something? I am telling you. I know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe it's, it's, it's, it's, it's good people. Maybe here it's good people, like even in Europe was-- Polish people was not bad. Maybe here is good. But there is plenty, plenty. You know, when I talk, I can't talk this to my son. He don't want to hear. Don't give me a headache. I know everything. Don't give me a headache. I know how it was. I know how it was two years in the woods. I know about the year in the ghetto. You know, once I remember I went to work with the-- the Germans chased the girl to work, everybody. I went to work. Listen, with-- everybody took a piece of bread or something to work, you know. Then lunchtime, the German gave us a half-hour lunchtime.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember I was sitting. And three little girls-- there was three cousins of them. And then a Ukrainian lady came and sat down near us. And she take out food and eat, you know? We didn't talk to her. We didn't got money to buy something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then a German came. Listen. A German came and asked the Ukrainian lady if the, the girls wants to buy food by you. She make with her head like this. The girls had-- we have no money. We don't buy nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: He took all the three girls and shot them right away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You like it? That's all. I saw this. Finished. OK. Thank you very much--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother's brother, Baruch, the first name. The second name is Schanwalt. I don't know when he is born. I don't know. I, I wasn't born in that time. The picture, I suppose, is taken in Luboml, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you when it was taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's my cousin. She's my girlfriend. The name is Gula Koltun. The picture was taken in Luboml by [NON-ENGLISH]. That was the photographer, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next to her? The next one is my girlfriend. Her name is [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is she in the photograph?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, where is she in the photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the middle, the next one.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. The second name is [NON-ENGLISH]. She's from Luboml.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who, who is on top?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was that time, I don't remember. 14 or 15-- we was 14,15 year old, yeah. Here, that's my husband. He was a young boy. I didn't know him that time. And the name is Chaim. The real name and in Luboml was [NON-ENGLISH]. He changed the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is he in the photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first one. The left, yeah? That's what he told me.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to show? I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. And the next? Who is-- who is next--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a cousin. The name is Lebel. Wait a minute. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is next to him? We'll, we'll come back. Who is next to him on the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schrulek, Schrulek Kramer, this I know. I remember the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is on the bottom?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the bottom is a cousin. The first name is Chaim, too, I think-- Chaim Visztnitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm sorry. Should I start it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my first husband, Lebel Horen. And the picture was taken in Displaced Person camp, I remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- I don't know. I think '47. He was at there three years until '49. Three years we been there. Yeah, that's my little baby, my son, Suchar. Sam-- now we call him Sam. This was the-- it was in the first-- the Passover. He was a year and a half when we came to America, Brooklyn. I remember, yeah. My cousin took the picture, I remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, that's my, my son. He was a year-- he was, when we came with a ship in New Orleans, it was 1949. And they took pictures. They show-- there was a lot of people that time. And they took them. They never saw a little boy with a-- with a fur, fur, [LAUGHS] fur coat. And they took him. He was at that time a year and a half in New Orleans, yeah. Yeah, this is my second husband, Chaim. I think he told he took the picture after the war when he came home from this-- from, from that-- from the camp where he was in the-- in the war, Sobibor. I think, this is the first year. Had told me he took the picture when he came from, from-- there was a camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, it was-- it's Posner. But really, in Europe, it was-- he changed the name. It was [NON-ENGLISH]. Yeah, that's me with my family. That's me, my son, Sam Horen, his wife, Pauline, and the older son Lee, and the younger, Jeremy. It's three years ago. We took the-- by the bar mitzvah for the older son. Now, he's 16. OK, that's me with my two grandsons. It's taken three years ago by the bar mitzvah. The older one was bar mitzvah. At that time, he was 13. But now, he's 16. And the younger one now is 10 year old. It's three years ago.
